A friend of mine, Sandy, is a highly successful divorce attorney. Often she serves as a mediator between divorcing parties who want to agree on the terms of their divorce without the time and expense of a courtroom trial. Before one of Sandy's mediations begins in her offices, partners are taken to separate rooms to avoid the shouting matches that can occur when the contestants are in the same physical space. Each side has already submitted a written proposal to Sandy, who shuttles between the two rooms seeking compromises to produce final terms that both partners will sign. She wondered if, as a psychologist, I could help with a frequently fatal deadlock that surfaces near the very end of many negotiations and is so resistant to compromise, it can torpedo the entire mediation process and send the company into divorce court. The deadlock can be on a major issue or it can be on a minor one. No matter, the combatants dig in their heels and refuse to budge in any meaningful way on this last piece of the agreement. I asked Sandy what she normally says to disputants in this situation. She answered that she takes the last offer on the issue from one room to the other, presents it and says, all you have to do is agree to this and we will have a deal. I thought I recognized the problem and suggested a minor wording change too. We have a deal. All you have to do is agree to this. Several months later, at a party, Sandy walked up, bearing a wide smile and told me the changed wording had been amazingly successful. She said, it works every time. Now I was skeptical, so I replied, come on, every time she put her hand on my arm and she said, Bob, every time. Now, although I remain skeptical about its 100% success rate, we're talking about behavioral science here, not magic. I was certainly pleased with the effectiveness of the recommended change. Truthfully though, I wasn't surprised. I had made the suggestion because of what I knew about the process of loss of version, especially under conditions of uncertainty. People are intensely motivated to make choices designed to avoid losing something of value to a much greater extent than they're motivated to make choices designed to obtain that same thing. This general idea that people are more driven by the prospect of losing an item of value than by the prospect of gaining it has received support from studies done all over the world. Notice that in keeping with the idea of loss aversion, the wording change I proposed to Sandy began by assigning to her clients possession of something they wanted. We have a deal which they would lose if they failed to compromise. Contrast that with Sandy's original approach in which the desired deal was something only to be gained. We agree to this point and we will have a deal. Knowing what I knew, the wording adjustment was an easy one for me to suggest. If you ever find yourself in a negotiation with one last sticking point, I hope you'll now know to engage the power of loss aversion too by announcing something like, we've got a deal. If you can agree to this last change, we're done here.
